title: Multivariate Conformal Prediction using Optimal Transport

reviews.summary: This paper proposes OT-CP, a conformal prediction method for multivariate outputs that uses optimal-transport-based center-outward ranks (Kantorovich ranks) to create an ordering of vector-valued nonconformity scores. The method maps vector scores to the unit ball via an estimated (entropic) OT map and uses the radial coordinate as a scalar score for conformal calibration, yielding distribution-free marginal coverage and, empirically, smaller prediction regions than common multivariate CP baselines. The paper provides a finite-sample coverage argument relying on exchangeability, a practical implementation via entropic maps computed with Sinkhorn, and an extensive empirical evaluation on a broad benchmark of multivariate regression tasks.
strengths: Leverages center-outward distributions and Kantorovich ranks to induce an order on multivariate scores, a long-standing challenge in multivariate CP.
The “OT merging” perspective is conceptually simple and unifies with existing scalar-score CP; the link to Merge-CP and Mahalanobis norms as special cases (under Gaussian/uniform) is insightful.
Provides a practical out-of-sample, differentiable OT map via entropic regularization to scale the approach beyond discrete assignment.
Broad evaluation across 20+ multivariate datasets, with ablations over key hyperparameters (entropic regularization ε and target grid size m).
Reports both marginal coverage and region size, and includes runtime analysis highlighting computational trade-offs.
Good high-level motivation of the difficulty of ranking multivariate scores and why OT-based center-outward ranks are appropriate.
Implementation details (entropic map, sphere discretization, low-discrepancy directions) are explained sufficiently to enable reproduction in principle.
Addresses an important gap: principled construction of multivariate conformal sets without strong generative assumptions.
Offers a flexible framework that can be plugged into a range of base predictors and could stimulate further work on fast/approximate OT maps for CP.
weaknesses: The finite-sample validity ultimately follows from standard conformal principles once a scalar score is defined; beyond adopting OT-based ranks, the theoretical novelty of the coverage guarantee is limited. The claim of a “first calibration guarantee” for such regions feels overstated given that CP yields distribution-free coverage with any scalar score function.

The coverage argument in Section 3.3/Proposition 3.4 mixes an empirical pushforward radius rα,n+1 and the transformed norm but is not fully precise about whether rα,n+1 depends on the candidate response (transductive CP) or is fit on a calibration set (split CP). This ambiguity obscures the exact validity regime.

OT map estimation is subject to the curse of dimensionality; while the method maintains coverage, practical efficiency and region tightness degrade in higher dimensions, as partially reflected in the experiments.

Missing comparisons to relevant multivariate CP baselines that rely on probabilistic models, e.g., CD-split and HPD-split (Izbicki et al., 2022), or copula/energy-based multivariate CP methods beyond a brief discussion; this makes it hard to position the empirical effectiveness against the broader literature.

The evaluation of region size relies on importance sampling from MQF²; meanwhile M-CP leverages MQF² to build per-dimension intervals, while OT-CP uses only a conditional mean. The study would benefit from clarifying and controlling model capacities across methods to ensure an apples-to-apples comparison.

Inconsistent notation and occasional typos/formatting artifacts (e.g., references with “?”, self-referential figure caption “Figure 6…Figure 6”, occasional confusion about α = 5% vs a dashed line at 0.8 target coverage).

The exact choice of the vector-valued score S(x,y) for OT-CP is not crisply stated in the main text (is it y − ŷ(x)?), nor are the data splits (train/calibration/test) and whether the OT map is fit only on training/calibration or includes test candidate dependence.
Lack of an algorithm box or pseudocode leaves important operational details (e.g., how candidate-dependent regions are computed) to inference by the reader.

Limited discussion of data depth–based rankings for multivariate CP and related rank-transforms (e.g., center-outward ranks in inference tasks) and how OT-CP compares in computational and statistical properties.

No comparison to flow-based predictive regions that directly target minimum-volume sets under learned densities, or to multivariate predictive region methods using energy scores or highest-density regions (HPD).
reviews.questions:
1. Please clarify precisely how the OT map T̂ is trained and used with respect to the data splits. Is T̂ fit only on training/calibration nonconformity scores and fixed at test time? If Proposition 3.4 uses rα,n+1 computed from Pn+1, does this imply a fully transductive (candidate-dependent) procedure? A concise algorithm box would help.
2. What is the exact vector-valued score S(x,y) used in OT-CP across experiments (e.g., residual y − ŷ(x))? If so, what base model ŷ is used, and is it the same across methods to ensure fairness?
3. Could you include baselines such as CD-split and HPD-split (Izbicki et al., 2022) and, if feasible, copula-based multivariate CP methods, to better position OT-CP among probabilistic multivariate approaches?
4. How sensitive are the results to the choice of m and ε across datasets? Do you have a principled or data-driven tuning heuristic (e.g., via calibration folds) that balances runtime and region size while maintaining coverage?
5. The region size is estimated by importance sampling using MQF². How robust are the size comparisons if the flow is misspecified? Can you provide an alternative, model-agnostic proxy for region size on at least a subset of datasets?
6. Proposition 3.5 provides a closed-form rα for the discrete spherical grid. Could you supply a brief proof or derivation in the appendix and discuss how approximate assignment (i.e., approximate Tn+1) affects the realized coverage?
7. There are several inconsistencies/typos (e.g., “?” in references, Figure 6 referencing itself, mixed α targets). Can you revise to ensure clarity, and explicitly report the target α for each figure?
reviews.assessment: This paper tackles an important and challenging problem—constructing distribution-free conformal sets for multivariate outputs—by importing center-outward ranks from optimal transport to define a principled ordering of vector-valued scores. The idea is elegant, and the implementation via entropic maps is practical and well-explained. Empirically, OT-CP often yields tighter regions than standard aggregations (M-CP boxes; Euclidean/Mahalanobis norms) at similar coverage, with transparent ablations and runtime reporting. However, the theoretical contribution beyond standard conformal validity (which holds for any scalar score) is modest, some core algorithmic details need clarification (particularly the exact calibration protocol and dependence on candidate responses), and key multivariate CP baselines are missing. Writing issues and minor inconsistencies also detract from clarity. Overall, I find the paper promising and of interest to the ICML community, but I recommend revision to sharpen the methodological description, broaden comparisons, and temper some claims. With those improvements, this work could make a solid contribution to multivariate conformal prediction.
